pattern: Instead of touching every stored element on every bulk add/multiply, track a single running affine transform (multiplier, offset) applied to the whole sequence and store each appended value in the coordinate space that existed at the moment it was appended; a query replays only that element's own catch-up transform.
hints: Doing addAll/multAll by looping over every stored element is correct but too slow when both operations and queries can number in the hundred-thousands; find a way to make append, addAll, and multAll all O(1).

Keep two running numbers, a global multiplier and a global additive offset (both modulo 1e9+7), that represent 'apply this transform to everything currently in the sequence'. addAll only updates the offset; multAll updates both the multiplier and the offset. Store each newly appended value pre-adjusted so that applying the CURRENT transform to it reproduces the value it should have right now.

On append(val), the value to store is (val - currentOffset) * inverse(currentMultiplier) mod 1e9+7 (using Fermat's little theorem for the modular inverse, since 1e9+7 is prime) — that stored value, transformed later, always yields the correct live value. getIndex(idx) simply returns (stored[idx] * multiplier + offset) mod 1e9+7, or -1 if idx is out of range.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: interleaved append/addAll/multAll with getIndex checks at several points
getIndex called with idx exactly equal to the current length (one past the last valid index) returns -1
getIndex on an empty sequence always returns -1
addAll/multAll applied before any elements exist must not affect elements appended afterwards
an element appended between two multAll calls only picks up the transform accumulated since its own append, never the earlier one
repeated multAll by 1 and addAll by nothing-changing values must be a true no-op on stored values
many multAll calls in a row on a single early element stresses the modular multiplier growth and inverse computation

stress.description: long randomized mix of append/addAll/multAll/getIndex exercising the running transform across many elements added at different times
stress.note: idx is occasionally sampled just past length to also exercise the -1 path
